(27 October 1763 – 23 March 1840) was an Americanized [Scottish](/wiki/Scot "Scot") [geologist](/wiki/Geologist "Geologist"), cartographer and philanthropist.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChisholm1911-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) He is known as the 'father of American geology'.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) As a social experimenter on new types of community life, he collaborated with British [social reformer](/wiki/Social_reform "Social reform") [Robert Owen](/wiki/Robert_Owen "Robert Owen"), (1771–1854), in the utopian settlement of [New Harmony](/wiki/New_Harmony,_Indiana "New Harmony, Indiana") in [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana"), United States.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

18th/19th-century American geologist and cartographer

| William Maclure | |
| --- | --- |
| William Maclure | |
| Born | 27 October 1763  [Ayr](/wiki/Ayr "Ayr"), Scotland |
| Died | 23 March 1840(1840-03-23) (aged 76)  [San Ángel](/wiki/San_%C3%81ngel "San Ángel"), Mexico |
| Citizenship | American |
| Known for | First geological map of America 1809, and [New Harmony Society](/wiki/New_Harmony,_Indiana "New Harmony, Indiana") |
| **Scientific career** | |
| Fields | [geology](/wiki/Geology "Geology"), [education](/wiki/Education "Education"), [philanthropy](/wiki/Philanthropy "Philanthropy") |
| Institutions | [Academy of Natural Sciences of Philadelphia](/wiki/Academy_of_Natural_Sciences_of_Philadelphia "Academy of Natural Sciences of Philadelphia") |
|  | |

**William Maclure** (27 October 1763 – 23 March 1840) was an Americanized [Scottish](/wiki/Scot "Scot") [geologist](/wiki/Geologist "Geologist"), cartographer and philanthropist.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChisholm1911-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) He is known as the 'father of American geology'.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) As a social experimenter on new types of community life, he collaborated with British [social reformer](/wiki/Social_reform "Social reform") [Robert Owen](/wiki/Robert_Owen "Robert Owen"), (1771–1854), in the utopian settlement of [New Harmony](/wiki/New_Harmony,_Indiana "New Harmony, Indiana") in [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana"), United States.

Maclure had a highly successful mercantile career, making a fortune that allowed him to retire in 1797 at the early age of 34 to pursue his scientific, [geological](/wiki/Geological "Geological") and other interests. In 1809 he made the earliest attempt at a [geological map](/wiki/Geological_map "Geological map") of the United States of America.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Maclure&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

### Early life, business, and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Maclure&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early life, business, and education")]

Maclure was born in 1763 in [Ayr](/wiki/Ayr "Ayr"), Scotland.

After a brief visit to New York City in 1782, he began work with the merchants Miller, Hart & Co, who traded and shipped goods to and from America. Maclure was based in the London office but regularly travelled to France and Ireland on business.[[6]](#cite_note-6) In 1796 business affairs took him to [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia"), which he thereafter made his home.
In 1803 he visited France as one of the commissioners appointed to settle the claims of American citizens on the French government; and during the few years then spent in Europe he applied himself with enthusiasm to the study of [geology](/wiki/Geology "Geology").[[7]](#cite_note-EB1911-7)
While residing in Switzerland, he became impressed with what is now called the Pestalozzi School System, from Swiss pedagogist [Johann Heinrich Pestalozzi](/wiki/Johann_Heinrich_Pestalozzi "Johann Heinrich Pestalozzi") (1746–1827).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Geological map

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Maclure&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Geological map")]

Maclure visit to Spain in 1808; this visit ended abruptly by the outbreak of the Peninsular War. After his return home in 1808 he commenced the self-imposed task of making a geological survey of the United States. Almost every state in the Union was traversed and mapped by him, the [Allegheny Mountains](/wiki/Allegheny_Mountains "Allegheny Mountains") being crossed and recrossed some 50 times.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The results of his unaided labours were submitted to the [American Philosophical Society](/wiki/American_Philosophical_Society "American Philosophical Society") in a memoir entitled *Observations on the Geology of the United States explanatory of a Geological Map*, and published in the Society's Transactions, together with the first geological map of that country,[[7]](#cite_note-EB1911-7) [Maclure's 1809 Geological Map](http://www.davidrumsey.com/luna/servlet/detail/RUMSEY~8~1~959~60260:A-Map-of-the-United-States-of-Ameri?sort=Pub_Date%2CPub_List_No%2CSeries_No&qvq=q:Geology;sort:Pub_Date%2CPub_List_No%2CSeries_No;lc:RUMSEY~8~1&mi=2&trs=1282).[[9]](#cite_note-9) This antedates [William Smith](/wiki/William_Smith_(geologist) "William Smith (geologist)")'s geological map of England and Wales (with part of Scotland) by six years, although it was constructed using a different classification of rocks.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Maclure_Geological_Map_Transactions_1817.jpg/250px-Maclure_Geological_Map_Transactions_1817.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maclure_Geological_Map_Transactions_1817.jpg)

Maclure's Geological Map of the United States, published in 1817

In 1812, while in France, Maclure became a member of the newly founded [Academy of Natural Sciences of Philadelphia](/wiki/Academy_of_Natural_Sciences "Academy of Natural Sciences") (ANSP). In 1817 Maclure became president of the ANSP, a post he held for the next 22 years.[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10)

In 1817, while residing in Europe, Maclure brought before the same society a revised edition of his map, and his great geological memoir, which he had issued separately, with some additional matter, under the title *Observations on the Geology of the United States of America*.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) Subsequent surveys have corroborated the general accuracy of Maclure's observations.[[7]](#cite_note-EB1911-7)

### Later years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Maclure&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Later years")]

In 1820 he visited Spain, because since the restoration of the Constitution in July 1820, Spain was the only liberal country in Europe. He attempted to establish an agricultural college near the city of [Alicante](/wiki/Alicante "Alicante"), but in 1823 the invasion of the French army, the [Hundred Thousand Sons of Saint Louis](/wiki/Hundred_Thousand_Sons_of_Saint_Louis "Hundred Thousand Sons of Saint Louis"), imposed absolutism again and on 2 May 1824, he abandoned Alicante.[[10]](#cite_note-:0-10) Returning to America in 1824, he settled for some years at [New Harmony, Indiana](/wiki/New_Harmony,_Indiana "New Harmony, Indiana"), seeking to develop his vision of the agricultural college. Failing health ultimately required him to relinquish the attempt and to seek (in 1827) a more congenial climate in Mexico.[[7]](#cite_note-EB1911-7) There, in 1840, at [San Ángel](/wiki/San_%C3%81ngel "San Ángel"), he died aged 77. His [will](/wiki/Last_will "Last will") provided for a [trust fund](/wiki/Trust_fund "Trust fund") consisting of most of his property. Under the terms of the Trust, 160 workingmen's libraries were established. The treatment of Maclure's burial site in Mexico was bereft of the honors due the respected humanitarian and geologist.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The burial site of William Maclure

> At the distance of a few feet from them, repose the remains of William McClure, a countryman, dear to American science. The Academy of Natural Sciences at Philadelphia, of which he was so long the President and benefactor, erected a small marble monument over his grave, and surrounded it with an iron rail. A short time before I left Mexico, the rail was torn down, the monument upset, and, on the same night, the newly-buried body of a Scotchman was disinterred, stripped of its clothes, and thrown over the wall of the cemetery!

[Brantz Mayer](/wiki/Brantz_Mayer "Brantz Mayer")[[14]](#cite_note-14)

* **Summary of the second phase of Maclure's life** (after Moore 1947)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## New Harmony

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Maclure&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: New Harmony")]

Main article: [New Harmony, Indiana](/wiki/New_Harmony,_Indiana "New Harmony, Indiana")

The New Harmony [commune](/wiki/Intentional_community "Intentional community") in Indiana was a short-lived [utopian](/wiki/Utopia "Utopia") community. Even after its collapse, it became home to geologists, naturalists, and botanists who were influenced by Maclure. This included the siblings [Robert Dale Owen](/wiki/Robert_Dale_Owen "Robert Dale Owen") (1801–1877), social reformer; [David Dale Owen](/wiki/David_Dale_Owen "David Dale Owen") (1807–1860), geologist, artist; Jane Dale Owen Fauntleroy (1806–1861), educator; and [Richard Dale Owen](/wiki/Richard_Dale_Owen "Richard Dale Owen") (1810–1890) geologist, first president of [Purdue University](/wiki/Purdue_University "Purdue University"), [Lafayette, Indiana](/wiki/Lafayette,_Indiana "Lafayette, Indiana"). A formidable group of geologists, social reformers, [botanists](/wiki/Botanist "Botanist"), [paleobotanists](/wiki/Paleobotany "Paleobotany"), [ethnologists](/wiki/Ethnology "Ethnology") and [civil engineers](/wiki/Civil_engineering "Civil engineering") interacted at New Harmony.

## Published works

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Maclure&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Published works")]

* Maclure, W. 1817. Observations on the geology of the West India Islands, from Barbadoes to Santa Cruz, inclusive. *Journal of the Academy of Natural Sciences of Philadelphia* 1(6), 134–149. ([BHL link](https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/79416#page/178/mode/1up))
* Maclure, W. 1818. Essay on the formation of rocks, or an inquiry into the probable origin of their present form and structure. Part 1. *Journal of the Academy of Natural Sciences of Philadelphia* 1(7), 261–276. ([BHL link](https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/79416#page/324/mode/1up))
* Maclure, W. 1818. Essay on the formation of rocks, or an inquiry into the probable origin of their present form and structure. Part 2. *Journal of the Academy of Natural Sciences of Philadelphia* 1(7), 285–310. ([BHL link](https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/79416#page/324/mode/1up))
* Maclure, W. 1818. Essay on the formation of rocks, or an inquiry into the probable origin of their present form and structure. Part 3. *Journal of the Academy of Natural Sciences of Philadelphia* 1(7), 327–345. ([BHL link](https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/79416#page/393/mode/1up))
* Maclure, W. 1818. On the geology of the United States of North America, with remarks on the probable effects that may be produced by the decomposition of the different classes of rocks, on the nature and fertility of soils: applied to the different states of the Union agreeably to the accompanying geological map. *Transactions of the American Philosophical Society* new series 1, 1–92. ([BHL link](https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/80765#page/33/mode/1up))

## Primary sources

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Maclure&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Primary sources")]

*The European Journals of William Maclure*[[16]](#cite_note-16) was a monumental book, describing, charting, and chronicling much of the features of Europe.

## Taxonomic eponyms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Maclure&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Taxonomic eponyms")]

* [Charles Alexandre Lesueur](/wiki/Charles_Alexandre_Lesueur "Charles Alexandre Lesueur") described a genus of fossil [gastropods](/wiki/Gastropod "Gastropod"), *[Maclurites](/w/index.php?title=Maclurites&action=edit&redlink=1 "Maclurites (page does not exist)")*, in honor of Maclure.[[17]](#cite_note-17) It is the type genus of [Macluritidae](/wiki/Macluritidae "Macluritidae"), an extinct family of relatively large gastropods known from the [Lower Ordovician](/wiki/Lower_Ordovician "Lower Ordovician") to [Devonian](/wiki/Devonian "Devonian") periods.[[18]](#cite_note-Smith1899-18) Two other members of the family, *[Maclurina](/w/index.php?title=Maclurina&action=edit&redlink=1 "Maclurina (page does not exist)")* and *[Macluritella](/w/index.php?title=Macluritella&action=edit&redlink=1 "Macluritella (page does not exist)")*, also honor Maclure.
* *[Maclura](/wiki/Maclura "Maclura")*, a genus of [flowering plants](/wiki/Flowering_plant "Flowering plant") in the [mulberry](/wiki/Morus_(plant) "Morus (plant)") family, [Moraceae](/wiki/Moraceae "Moraceae") is named in honor of Maclure. The genus includes the inedible [Osage-orange](/wiki/Maclura_pomifera "Maclura pomifera"), which is used as [mosquito](/wiki/Mosquito "Mosquito") [repellent](/wiki/Insect_repellent "Insect repellent") and grown throughout the United States as a [hedging plant](/wiki/Hedge "Hedge").[[19]](#cite_note-Silvics-19)

## Geological eponyms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Maclure&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Geological eponyms")]

* [Mount Maclure](/wiki/Mount_Maclure "Mount Maclure") in [Yosemite National Park](/wiki/Yosemite_National_Park "Yosemite National Park").[[20]](#cite_note-Farquhar-20)
* [Maclure Glacier](/wiki/Maclure_Glacier "Maclure Glacier"), one of the last remaining glaciers in Yosemite National Park.[[21]](#cite_note-topo-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)
